title: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use
authors: Sarah Meyer, Amanda Cohn, Georgina Peacock

items.subtopic: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT
items.title: Understanding Herd Immunity Thresholds for Vaccination Programs
items.content: Herd immunity is a critical concept in vaccination programs, where the level of immunization required to protect unvaccinated individuals varies by disease. High transmissibility diseases like measles require higher coverage rates compared to others.
keyPoints: Measles requires an estimated 95% population vaccination rate for herd immunity.
Polio requires an estimated 80% population vaccination rate for herd immunity.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT

items.subtopic: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT
items.title: Impact of Vaccination on Public Health Outcomes
items.content: Vaccination has significantly reduced the incidence of vaccine-preventable diseases, leading to substantial public health benefits including decreased illness rates and economic savings.
keyPoints: Vaccination against Streptococcus pneumoniae in children <5 years resulted in a 96% reduction in invasive pneumococcal disease.
Childhood vaccination series for those born during 1994–2023 will prevent 508 million illnesses and 1,129,000 deaths over their lifetimes.
Projected societal cost savings from these vaccinations is nearly $2.7 trillion (U.S.).
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Disease Control Goals
items.content: Immunization programs aim to control, eliminate, or eradicate vaccine-preventable diseases. Control involves reducing poor outcomes and limiting disruptions from outbreaks, while elimination requires zero cases in a defined area.
keyPoints: Control reduces illness outcomes and community disruption.
Elimination means zero indigenous transmission in a geographic area.
As of 2024, the U.S. eliminated measles, rubella, poliomyelitis, and diphtheria.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.title: Eradication Achievements and Challenges
items.content: Eradication is sustained elimination without ongoing interventions. Smallpox is the only globally eradicated human disease. Polio eradication efforts continue, with wild poliovirus type 1 endemic in Afghanistan and Pakistan.
keyPoints: Smallpox is the only globally eradicated vaccine-preventable disease.
Wild poliovirus types 2 and 3 are eradicated; type 1 persists in Afghanistan and Pakistan.
Detection of targeted diseases is a sentinel event requiring prompt reporting.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Public Health Reporting
items.content: Reporting suspected cases of diseases targeted for elimination or eradication is crucial. It enables urgent interventions like contact tracing and epidemiologic investigations.
keyPoints: Suspected cases must be reported to public health authorities.
Measles, pertussis, and Haemophilus influenzae type b are nationally notifiable.
Prompt reporting enhances outbreak control effectiveness.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)
items.title: Impact of COVID-19 on Global Vaccination Coverage
items.content: The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted routine vaccination programs worldwide, leading to declines in vaccine coverage. While some recovery has been noted, significant gaps remain for certain vaccines.
keyPoints: Vaccinations prevent 3.5–5 million deaths annually globally.
COVID-19 caused declines in vaccine coverage across more than 100 countries.
DPT coverage nearly recovered to 2019 levels by 2022.
22 million children missed their first dose of measles vaccine in 2022, compared with 19 million in 2019.
"The Big Catch-Up" initiative aims to restore childhood vaccination rates to at least prepandemic levels.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)
items.title: ACIP Recommendations for Adult Immunizations
items.content: The ACIP provides various recommendations for adult vaccinations, including routine, catch-up, risk-based, and shared clinical decision-making approaches.
keyPoints: Routine, catch-up, and risk-based recommendations target specific age or risk groups (e.g., recombinant zoster vaccination for adults ≥50 years).
Shared clinical decision-making involves personalized decisions based on individual characteristics and preferences (e.g., HPV vaccination for adults 27–45 years).
ACIP consists of up to 20 voting members and ex officio representatives from federal agencies.
Recommendations are available at www.cdc.gov/acip-recs/hcp/vaccine-specific/.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.title: Who Should Be Vaccinated and How Quickly?
items.content: Efforts should be made to ensure adults receive all indicated vaccines as quickly as possible. Assessing immunization history at every clinical encounter helps identify needed vaccinations according to the current adult immunization schedule.
keyPoints: Assess immunization status at each clinical encounter
Use decision-support tools in electronic health records for vaccination prompts
Standing orders can facilitate routine vaccines like influenza and zoster
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.title: Contraindications vs. Precautions Before Vaccination
items.content: Before administering vaccines, screen for contraindications that increase the risk of serious adverse reactions and precautions that may affect vaccine efficacy or safety.
keyPoints: A history of anaphylactic reaction to a vaccine component is a contraindication
Precautions may lead to deferral but not necessarily contraindication
Moderate/severe acute illness is generally a transient precaution
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.title: Handling Immediate Hypersensitivity to Vaccine Components
items.content: Severe allergic reactions like anaphylaxis to vaccine components are contraindications. Common allergens include egg protein, gelatin, and yeast.
keyPoints: Anaphylaxis to a vaccine component is a contraindication
Egg protein, gelatin, and yeast are common severe allergy triggers
Vaccines in vials or syringes with natural rubber latex should be avoided for those with anaphylactic latex allergies
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Vaccination Recommendations for Pregnant Persons
items.content: Pregnant persons should receive specific vaccines to protect both themselves and their infants. Tdap is recommended during each pregnancy between the 27th and 36th week of gestation, ideally earlier in this period, to prevent pertussis in infants. Influenza vaccination is advised annually for everyone aged ≥6 months, including pregnant persons in any trimester, preferably in September or October. COVID-19 vaccination is recommended for all individuals aged ≥6 months, including during pregnancy. RSV vaccine should be administered between the 32nd and 36th week of gestation to protect infants from severe RSV disease.
keyPoints: Tdap: Recommended during each pregnancy at 27th through 36th week of gestation (preferably earlier).
Influenza vaccine: Annually for everyone aged ≥6 months, including pregnant persons in any trimester; ideally September or October.
COVID-19 vaccination: Recommended for all individuals aged ≥6 months, including during pregnancy.
RSV vaccine: Administered to pregnant persons between 32nd and 36th week of gestation.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Vaccination Guidelines for Immunocompromised Persons
items.content: Immunocompromised individuals are at higher risk for severe infectious disease outcomes and require tailored vaccination strategies. They may need vaccines earlier than the general population, additional doses, or specific vaccines based on their condition. Live-virus vaccines are contraindicated in severely immunosuppressed persons due to the risk of vaccine virus replication leading to disseminated infection.
keyPoints: Immunocompromised individuals: Increased risk for severe infectious disease outcomes.
Vaccination timing and doses may differ (e.g., pneumococcal and zoster at age ≥19 years).
Live-virus vaccines are contraindicated in severely immunosuppressed persons.
MMR vaccine can be given to HIV-infected persons with CD4 percentage of ≥15% and count of ≥200/μL.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Vaccination Recommendations for Older Adults
items.content: Older adults experience immunosenescence, leading to increased susceptibility to infectious diseases and reduced vaccine responses. They should stay up-to-date with all recommended vaccines, including COVID-19 and Td/Tdap. Specific recommendations include zoster vaccine for those aged ≥50 years, pneumococcal conjugate vaccine for those aged ≥50 years, RSV vaccine for adults aged ≥75 years or 60–74 years at increased risk, and a higher-dose influenza vaccine for those aged ≥65 years.
keyPoints: Immunosenescence increases susceptibility to infectious diseases in older adults.
Zoster vaccine: Recommended for adults age ≥50 years.
Pneumococcal conjugate vaccine: For adults age ≥50 years.
RSV vaccine: For all adults aged ≥75 years and those 60–74 years at increased risk.
Higher-dose influenza vaccine recommended for adults age ≥65 years.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS
items.title: Vaccination Requirements for Health Care Workers
items.content: Health care workers are advised to stay current with vaccines based on age or underlying conditions, including influenza and COVID-19. They may need additional vaccines due to occupational exposure risks, such as the meningococcal vaccine for those handling Neisseria meningitidis isolates. Reporting of vaccination rates among health care personnel is part of quality reporting programs.
keyPoints: Health care workers must be up-to-date with recommended vaccines including influenza and COVID-19.
Additional vaccines may be required based on occupational exposure (e.g., meningococcal vaccine for laboratory personnel handling Neisseria meningitidis).
Acute-care hospitals report the proportion of health care personnel vaccinated against seasonal influenza and COVID-19 as part of quality reporting programs.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS
items.title: Vaccine Information Statements (VISs) for Adult Vaccines
items.content: A VIS is a one-page information sheet about the benefits and risks of vaccines, mandated by the National Childhood Vaccine Injury Act. For adults, VISs are required for vaccines covered under the Vaccine Injury Compensation Program, including tetanus, diphtheria, hepatitis A, B, HPV, influenza, MMR, pneumococcal conjugate, meningococcal, polio, and varicella. Combination vaccines require all relevant VISs to be provided.
keyPoints: VIS is a one-page information sheet about vaccine benefits and risks.
Mandated by the National Childhood Vaccine Injury Act for vaccines covered under the VICP.
Covered adult vaccines include tetanus, diphtheria, hepatitis A, B, HPV, influenza, MMR, pneumococcal conjugate, meningococcal, polio, and varicella.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS

items.subtopic: STORAGE AND HANDLING
items.title: Proper Reconstitution and Use Timing for Lyophilized Vaccines
items.content: Lyophilized vaccines, such as varicella, MMR, and recombinant zoster, require specific handling post-reconstitution. They must be protected from light, with varying time limits before administration: varicella within 30 minutes, recombinant zoster up to 6 hours, and MMR up to 8 hours.
keyPoints: Varicella vaccine must be administered within 30 min of reconstitution.
Recombinant zoster vaccine can be used up to 6 h after reconstitution.
MMR vaccine can be used up to 8 h after reconstitution.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · STORAGE AND HANDLING

items.subtopic: STORAGE AND HANDLING
items.title: Vaccine Storage Temperature Guidelines and Monitoring
items.content: Vaccines are stored at specific temperatures: refrigerator temperature (2–8°C) for inactivated vaccines, and freezer temperature (−15°C or colder) for lyophilized-powder live-virus vaccines. Continuous monitoring of storage unit temperatures is crucial to ensure vaccine efficacy.
keyPoints: Inactivated vaccines are stored at 2–8°C.
Lyophilized-powder live-virus vaccines are stored at −15°C or colder.
Temperature must be monitored and recorded at least twice each workday, ideally with continuous thermometers.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · STORAGE AND HANDLING

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.title: Choosing Needle Length for IM Vaccine Administration
items.content: The appropriate needle length for intramuscular (IM) vaccine administration in adults is determined by the recipient's sex and weight to ensure adequate muscle penetration.
keyPoints: For men and women weighing <152 lb (<70 kg), use a 1-inch needle.
For women weighing 152-200 lb (70-90 kg) and men weighing 152-260 lb (70-118 kg), use a 1- to 1.5-inch needle.
For women weighing >200 lb (>90 kg) and men weighing >260 lb (>118 kg), use a 1.5-inch needle.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.title: Simultaneous Administration of Multiple Vaccines
items.content: Multiple vaccines can be administered simultaneously without compromising efficacy or increasing adverse effects, and this approach is encouraged to improve vaccine uptake.
keyPoints: Vaccines are as effective when given simultaneously as individually.
No increased risk of adverse effects with simultaneous administration.
Separate injection sites by 1–2 inches if multiple vaccines in the same limb; use separate anatomic sites for vaccines and immune globulin preparations.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.title: Preventing Syncope Post-Vaccination
items.content: To reduce the risk of injury from syncope following vaccination, especially in adolescents and young adults, it is recommended to observe patients seated or lying down for 15 minutes post-vaccination.
keyPoints: Syncope may occur within 15 min after vaccination, particularly in adolescents and young adults.
Seated or lying position during vaccination can prevent injuries from syncope.
Observation for 15 min post-vaccination is recommended to manage potential syncope.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.title: Importance of Comprehensive Vaccine Record Documentation
items.content: All vaccines administered should be documented in the patient's medical record, including details such as date, vaccine name/abbreviation, lot number and manufacturer, administration site, VIS edition, and provider information. The use of two-dimensional bar codes on vials and syringes can improve accuracy.
keyPoints: Complete documentation includes date, vaccine name/abbreviation, lot number, manufacturer, administration site, VIS edition, and provider details.
Two-dimensional bar codes facilitate accurate data entry into electronic medical records.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.title: Phases of Prelicensure Vaccine Safety Evaluation
items.content: Vaccines undergo three phases of clinical trials before FDA licensure. Phase 1 involves <100 volunteers to evaluate safety and identify common adverse events; Phase 2 includes several hundred participants to assess safety and immunogenicity, determining vaccine composition and dosing; Phase 3 involves several hundred to thousands to demonstrate efficacy and gather further safety data.
keyPoints: Phase 1 trials involve <100 volunteers for basic safety evaluation.
Phase 2 trials may include several hundred participants to evaluate immunogenicity and determine vaccine composition and dosing.
Phase 3 trials typically involve several hundred to thousands of volunteers to demonstrate efficacy and gather additional safety data.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.title: Role and Limitations of VAERS in Postlicensure Vaccine Safety Monitoring
items.content: VAERS, established under the NCVIA of 1986, collects reports of adverse events post-vaccination. In 2019, it received over 48,000 reports, with 85-90% being mild side effects. Reports can be filed by various parties and are used by CDC and FDA for safety monitoring. However, VAERS has limitations as a passive system that cannot determine if an event occurs more frequently than expected.
keyPoints: VAERS collects adverse event reports post-vaccination; over 48,000 reports were received in 2019.
85-90% of VAERS reports describe mild side effects such as fever and arm soreness.
VAERS is a passive system with limitations in determining the frequency of events post-vaccination.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES
items.title: Financial Barriers and Policy Changes in Adult Vaccination Access
items.content: The Affordable Care Act of 2010 and the Inflation Reduction Act of 2023 have significantly reduced financial barriers to adult vaccination by eliminating out-of-pocket costs for vaccines covered under private insurance, Medicare prescription drug coverage, and state Medicaid programs. However, uninsured adults still face challenges in accessing vaccinations.
keyPoints: Affordable Care Act (2010) requires most private insurance plans to cover immunizations without deductibles or copays when administered by an in-network provider.

Inflation Reduction Act (2023) eliminated out-of-pocket costs for ACIP-recommended vaccines for patients with Medicare prescription drug coverage and mandates state Medicaid programs to cover these vaccines without cost-sharing.
Barriers remain for uninsured adults; a proposed Vaccines for Adults program has not been funded.
CDC's Bridge Access Program provided COVID-19 vaccines to uninsured and underinsured adults from 2023 to 2024.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES

items.subtopic: CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES
items.title: Enhancing Access Through Non-Traditional Venues and Health Promotion
items.content: Improving access to vaccinations involves extending office hours, utilizing pharmacies, and leveraging non-traditional venues like occupational clinics and retail settings. These strategies address both logistical barriers and aim to increase consumer demand by raising awareness about adult vaccination needs.
keyPoints: Extended office hours (evening/weekend) and scheduled vaccination-only clinics reduce waiting times.
Pharmacies, located within 5 miles of the majority of adults, play a crucial role in improving vaccine access.
Non-traditional venues like occupational clinics, universities, and retail settings expand access for infrequent medical visitors.
Health promotion efforts must be coupled with strategies to reduce consumer barriers to effectively increase immunization rates.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES

items.subtopic: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT
items.question: What is the estimated percentage of population coverage required to achieve herd immunity for measles?
items.explanation: An estimated 95% of the population needs to be vaccinated to achieve herd immunity against measles.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT

items.subtopic: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT
items.question: What is one direct effect of immunizations mentioned in the passage?
items.explanation: Immunizations protect individuals against infection and thereby prevent symptomatic illnesses.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT

items.subtopic: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT
items.question: What is the impact of vaccination on Streptococcus pneumoniae in children <5 years old?
options: No change in incidence among adults
items.explanation: Vaccination of children <5 years old against Streptococcus pneumoniae has led to a 96% reduction in invasive pneumococcal disease.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following diseases has been globally eradicated?
items.explanation: The only vaccine-preventable disease that has been globally eradicated is smallpox.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.question: What does the elimination of a disease typically require?
options: Reduction to zero cases in a defined geographic area
items.explanation: Elimination usually requires the reduction to zero of cases in a defined geographic area.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.question: What is considered a sentinel event in public health?
options: Detection of a case of disease targeted for eradication
items.explanation: A sentinel event is the detection of a case of disease that has been targeted for eradication or elimination.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.question: Which factor can lead to an increase in vaccine-preventable diseases?
items.explanation: Waning of vaccine-induced immunity can lead to an increase in vaccine-preventable diseases.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)
items.question: Which organization was NOT mentioned as part of 'The Big Catch-Up' initiative to support recovery of childhood vaccination rates?
options: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
items.explanation: 'The Big Catch-Up' includes WHO, UNICEF, Gavi, and the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)
items.question: What was the approximate number of children who missed their first dose of measles vaccine in 2022?
items.explanation: In 2022, approximately 22 million children missed their first dose of the measles vaccine.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)
items.question: What is a factor that can increase immunization coverage in adults?
options: Reducing the number of vaccines administered
items.explanation: Increasing immunization coverage is more effective with multiple strategies rather than a single strategy.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.question: Which of the following is considered a contraindication to vaccination?
options: A history of anaphylactic reaction to a vaccine component.
History of mild local reaction to a previous dose of the vaccine.
Moderate or severe acute illness with fever.
items.explanation: A history of anaphylactic reaction to a vaccine component is a contraindication for further doses.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.question: What should be done when a patient has a moderate or severe acute illness with fever?
options: Postpone vaccination until the acute phase resolves.
Vaccinate only if the patient consents.
items.explanation: Moderate or severe acute illness with fever is a precaution that results in postponement of vaccine administration.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.question: What role do standing orders play in adult immunization?
options: They require a physician's order for each vaccine.
They allow nurses to administer vaccines without specific physician orders.
They are used only for pediatric vaccinations.
They eliminate the need for electronic health records.
items.explanation: Standing orders permit a nurse or another approved licensed practitioner to administer vaccines without a specific physician order.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.question: Which of the following substances is most likely to cause severe allergic reactions in individuals?
items.explanation: Substances like egg protein, gelatin, and yeast are most likely to cause severe allergic reactions.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.question: During which gestational period is Tdap vaccination recommended for pregnant persons to prevent pertussis in young infants?
items.explanation: Tdap vaccine is recommended during each pregnancy at the 27th through 36th week of gestation.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.question: When should maternal RSV vaccination ideally be administered to pregnant persons?
options: 32nd through 36th week during September to January
items.explanation: Maternal RSV vaccination is recommended during the 32nd through 36th week of gestation, ideally from September to January.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.question: Which vaccine is contraindicated for pregnant persons due to the risk of congenital infection?
items.explanation: Live-virus vaccines, such as measles, mumps, and rubella (MMR), are contraindicated during pregnancy.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.question: Which vaccine is recommended for older adults aged ≥65 years to provide better protection against influenza?
options: High-dose, adjuvanted, or recombinant influenza vaccine
items.explanation: Older adults aged ≥65 years are recommended to receive a higher-dose influenza vaccine.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS
items.question: Which vaccine is NOT specifically required to have a Vaccine Information Statement (VIS) under the National Childhood Vaccine Injury Act (NCVIA)?
items.explanation: The NCVIA mandates VISs for vaccines covered by the Vaccine Injury Compensation Program (VICP), which does not include Japanese encephalitis.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS
items.question: What is a requirement for health care workers under the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services' Hospital Inpatient Quality Reporting program?
options: Receiving all vaccines recommended by age or underlying condition
Reporting the proportion of vaccinated personnel for seasonal influenza and COVID-19
Mandatory vaccination against hepatitis B, measles, mumps, rubella, and varicella
Providing VISs to patients before administering any vaccine
items.explanation: The program requires reporting the proportion of health care personnel who have received seasonal influenza and COVID-19 vaccination.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS
items.question: Which vaccines are covered by both the Vaccine Injury Compensation Program (VICP) and have a VIS under the NCVIA?
items.explanation: Hepatitis A, hepatitis B, and HPV vaccines are covered by the NCVIA and have a VIS.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS

items.subtopic: STORAGE AND HANDLING
items.question: Which of the following vaccines must be protected from light and administered within 30 minutes after reconstitution?
items.explanation: Varicella must be protected from light and administered within 30 minutes after reconstitution.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · STORAGE AND HANDLING

items.subtopic: STORAGE AND HANDLING
items.question: At what temperature range should vaccines generally be stored in a refrigerator?
items.explanation: Vaccines are generally stored at refrigerator temperatures of 2–8°C.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · STORAGE AND HANDLING

items.subtopic: STORAGE AND HANDLING
items.question: What type of storage unit does the CDC recommend for vaccine storage?
options: Stand-alone units that either refrigerate or freeze but not both
items.explanation: The CDC recommends stand-alone units for vaccine storage as they maintain required temperatures better.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · STORAGE AND HANDLING

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.question: Which route is recommended for administering most parenteral vaccines to adults in the United States?
items.explanation: Most parenteral vaccines are given by either the intramuscular (IM) or subcutaneous (SC) route.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.question: What is the recommended needle length for administering an IM vaccine to a man weighing 150 lb?
items.explanation: For men and women weighing <152 lb (<70 kg), a 1-inch needle is sufficient for IM administration.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.question: When administering multiple vaccines at the same visit, what should be done if more than one vaccine must be administered in the same limb?
options: Use separate limbs for each vaccine
items.explanation: Injection sites should be separated by 1–2 inches to differentiate local reactions.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.question: What is the recommended action if a patient develops syncope after vaccination?
items.explanation: Patients should be observed until the symptoms of syncope resolve.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.question: Which of the following must be included in a patient's permanent medical record for vaccines administered?
options: Date of administration, vaccine name or abbreviation, lot number and manufacturer, administration site, VIS edition, date VIS was provided, name, address, and title of the person who administered the vaccine
Only the type of vaccine and the date it was administered
The patient's medical history and previous vaccinations
items.explanation: All these details are required for complete documentation in a patient's permanent medical record.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.question: What is the purpose of using two-dimensional bar codes on vaccine vials and syringes?
options: To facilitate more complete and accurate recording of required information
To track the expiration date of vaccines
To monitor the temperature at which vaccines are stored
items.explanation: Two-dimensional bar codes help in scanning data into electronic medical records for accuracy.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.question: What is the primary purpose of Phase 1 vaccine trials?
options: To evaluate safety and identify common adverse events
To determine the composition of the vaccine
To collect data on long-term side effects
items.explanation: Phase 1 trials are small studies focused on basic safety evaluation.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.question: Which system was established in response to the NCVIA of 1986 for reporting adverse events following vaccination?
options: FDA's Biologics Effectiveness and Safety (BEST) system
items.explanation: VAERS was established to collect reports of adverse events following vaccination.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES
items.question: Which legislation requires most private insurance plans to cover immunizations without deductibles or copays when administered by an in-network provider?
items.explanation: The Affordable Care Act enacted in 2010 mandates coverage without deductibles or copays.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES

items.subtopic: CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES
items.question: What program launched by the CDC aimed to ensure access to COVID-19 vaccines for uninsured and underinsured adults in 2023?
items.explanation: The CDC launched the Bridge Access Program for COVID-19 vaccines in 2023.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES

items.subtopic: CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES
items.question: Which of the following has been proposed but not yet authorized for funding to provide vaccines to uninsured adults?
items.explanation: A comprehensive Vaccines for Adults program has been proposed but not yet authorized for funding.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES

items.subtopic: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT
items.statement: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need for improved adult vaccination infrastructure.
items.explanation: The passage mentions a need for improved infrastructure to ensure adult vaccination, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT

items.subtopic: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT
items.statement: Herd immunity requires 80% of the population to be vaccinated against measles.
items.explanation: The passage states that an estimated 95% of the population needs to be vaccinated for herd immunity against measles.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use | VACCINE IMPACT

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.statement: The United States has eliminated indigenous transmission of poliomyelitis.
items.explanation: As of 2024, the United States had eliminated indigenous transmission of poliomyelitis.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.statement: Endemic transmission of wild poliovirus type 1 continues globally.
items.explanation: Endemic transmission of wild poliovirus type 1 continues only in Afghanistan and Pakistan.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)
items.statement: The ACIP's recommendations are only for vaccines approved for use in children.
items.explanation: ACIP makes recommendations for vaccines approved or authorized by the FDA for both children and adults.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)
items.statement: 'The Big Catch-Up' initiative aims to restore childhood vaccination rates to at least prepandemic levels.
items.explanation: The initiative supports recovery of childhood vaccination rates to at least prepandemic levels.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.statement: A history of mild acute illness is a contraindication to vaccination.
items.explanation: In most cases, mild acute illness is not a contraindication to vaccination.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.statement: Local or contact hypersensitivity to latex in medical gloves is a contraindication for vaccines supplied in vials containing natural rubber latex.
items.explanation: Local or contact hypersensitivity to synthetic latex (in medical gloves) is not a contraindication.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.statement: Pregnancy is a contraindication for all types of vaccines.
items.explanation: Most inactivated vaccines are safe during pregnancy, and some are recommended.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.statement: Immunocompromised persons should receive live-virus vaccines as part of their routine immunization schedule.
items.explanation: Live-virus vaccines are contraindicated for severely immunocompromised individuals.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS
items.statement: VISs for COVID-19 vaccines are mandated under the National Childhood Vaccine Injury Act (NCVIA).
items.explanation: While VISs for COVID-19 vaccines exist, they are not specifically mandated by NCVIA but rather published under the PREP Act.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS
items.statement: VISs must be provided for any vaccine covered by the Vaccine Injury Compensation Program, regardless of recipient age.
items.explanation: The NCVIA mandates VISs for all vaccines covered by the VICP, whether given to children or adults.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 2) | VACCINE INFORMATION STATEMENTS

items.subtopic: STORAGE AND HANDLING
items.statement: Diluents for lyophilized vaccines can be stored at any temperature.
items.explanation: Diluents are specifically formulated and may be stored at refrigerator or room temperature, but not just any temperature.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · STORAGE AND HANDLING

items.subtopic: STORAGE AND HANDLING
items.statement: The CDC recommends using continuous thermometers to monitor vaccine storage temperatures.
items.explanation: Continuous thermometers that measure and record temperature all day and night are recommended by the CDC.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · STORAGE AND HANDLING

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.statement: Aspiration is necessary when administering vaccines to ensure no large blood vessels are present at the injection site.
items.explanation: Aspiration is not necessary because no large blood vessels are present at the recommended vaccine injection sites.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.statement: An interruption in a vaccination schedule requires restarting the entire series.
items.explanation: With some exceptions, an interruption does not require restarting of the entire series or adding extra doses.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.statement: A report to VAERS confirms that a vaccine caused an adverse event.
items.explanation: VAERS reports do not establish causation between vaccines and adverse events.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.statement: The Vaccine Safety Datalink project includes information on immunizations, medical conditions, demographics, laboratory results, and medication prescriptions.
items.explanation: This project collects comprehensive data from managed-care organizations.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES
items.statement: The Inflation Reduction Act requires state Medicaid programs to cover ACIP-recommended adult vaccines without cost-sharing.
items.explanation: As of 2023, the Inflation Reduction Act mandates this coverage.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES

items.subtopic: CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES
items.statement: Direct-to-consumer advertising alone has been sufficient to increase immunization rates.
items.explanation: Increased demand through advertising is effective only when combined with other strategies.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.assertion: The detection of a case of disease targeted for eradication requires prompt reporting to public health authorities.
items.reason: Prompt reporting can limit the forward spread of the infectious agent.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)
items.assertion: The COVID-19 pandemic demonstrated that vaccination programs are unnecessary.
items.reason: Robust surveillance systems were crucial during the pandemic.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; robust vaccination programs were essential, as highlighted by the pandemic.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · Control, Elimination, and Eradication of Vaccine-Preventable (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.assertion: A precaution may lead to deferral of vaccination.
items.reason: Precautions are conditions that increase the risk of an adverse event or compromise vaccine efficacy.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS
items.assertion: Vaccines should never be administered when a precaution is noted.
items.reason: The benefits of vaccination may outweigh the estimated risk in some cases.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true; vaccines can sometimes be given despite precautions.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · IMMUNIZATION PRACTICE STANDARDS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.assertion: Pregnant persons should receive the Tdap vaccine during each pregnancy.
items.reason: Tdap vaccination is recommended to prevent pertussis in young infants.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)
items.assertion: Older adults aged ≥50 years should receive RSV vaccine.
items.reason: RSV vaccine is recommended for all older adults to prevent severe outcomes from infectious diseases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; RSV vaccine is specifically recommended for those aged ≥75 and at increased risk among ages 60-74.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · SPECIFIC ADULT POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.assertion: Vaccines are as effective when administered simultaneously as they are individually.
items.reason: Simultaneous administration is not associated with an increased risk of adverse effects.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why vaccines can be effectively administered simultaneously.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES
items.assertion: All facilities providing immunizations should have an emergency kit containing aqueous epinephrine.
items.reason: Anaphylaxis is a common complication of vaccination.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because anaphylaxis is a rare complication.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · ADMINISTRATION OF VACCINES

items.subtopic: VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.assertion: VAERS is a passive reporting system.
items.reason: It cannot assess whether an event occurs more often than expected after vaccination.
items.explanation: The limitation of VAERS as a passive system supports the assertion.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING
items.assertion: Phase 2 vaccine trials are designed to evaluate immunogenicity.
items.reason: They collect additional information on safety and determine the number of doses required.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why Phase 2 evaluates immunogenicity.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · MAINTENANCE OF VACCINE RECORDS | VACCINE SAFETY MONITORING AND ADVERSE EVENT REPORTING

items.subtopic: CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES
items.assertion: Health care providers are the most trusted source of vaccine information.
items.reason: Patients who receive a strong vaccine recommendation from a provider are more likely to get vaccinated.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why health care providers influence vaccination rates.
items.reference: Immunization Principles and Vaccine Use · CONSUMER ACCESS TO AND DEMAND FOR IMMUNIZATION | STRATEGIES FOR PROVIDERS AND HEALTH CARE FACILITIES
